title: Define and call a method
instruction:
Static methods in C# are defined inside a class.

Run this.
successMessage: Methods package reusable behaviour. SayHello() called twice runs its body twice.

title: Parameters
instruction:
Parameters are declared with type and name.

Run this.
successMessage: Parameters make methods flexible. The caller controls which data gets processed.

title: Return values
instruction:
Methods can return a value with the `return` keyword. The return type is declared before the method name.

Run this.
successMessage: The return type (int, double) appears before the method name. void means 'returns nothing'.

title: Method overloading
instruction:
Multiple methods can share a name if they have different parameter types/counts.

Run this.
successMessage: The compiler chooses the right overload based on argument types. This is compile-time polymorphism.

title: Optional parameters
instruction:
Parameters can have default values — callers can omit them.

Run this.
successMessage: Optional parameters must come after required ones. Callers can supply them positionally or by name.

title: Named arguments
instruction:
Call with argument names to skip optionals or clarify code.

Run this.
successMessage: Named arguments make calls self-documenting and allow skipping optional parameters.

title: out parameters
instruction:
`out` lets a method return multiple values.

Run this.
successMessage: out parameters are declared at the call site with 'out'. TryParse uses this exact pattern.

title: Recursive methods
instruction:
Methods can call themselves. A base case prevents infinite recursion.

Run this.
successMessage: Each call reduces n by 1. When n reaches 1, recursion stops. Stack depth = n, so this is not suitable for very large n.
